title: Election Commission and Political Parties
focus: Covers the ECI's functions, registration of political parties, and the Special Intensive Revision of electoral rolls.
rationale: Fallback section derived from packet-level grouping.
sectionExcerpt:
[Page 039]
5. ELECTIONS
Election Commission of India (ECI)
Genesis:
- Permanent Constitutional Body established on 25th January 1950.
- Since 2011, National Voters' Day celebrated on January 25 every year to mark foundation day of ECI.
Constitutional Provision: Part XV of Constitution (Articles 324 to 329).
Statutory Provisions: The Chief Election Commissioner And Other Election Commissioners (Appointment, Conditions of Service And Term of Office) Act, 2023, regulates their appointment, service conditions, tenure, etc.
Key role: Administers elections to Lok Sabha, Rajya Sabha, State Legislative Assemblies, and Offices of President and Vice President. (Article 324(1))
Composition: Chief Election Commissioner (CEC) and two Election Commissioners (EC). (Article 324 (2))
Key functions:
- Supervises, directs, controls and conducts free and fair elections.
- Prepares electoral rolls for Lok Sabha and State Assemblies.
- Grants recognition to Political parties at national and state level.
- Determines and announces rules for fair and impartial elections.
Recent Steps Taken by ECI for Electoral Reforms:
- SVEEP (Systematic Voters' Education and Electoral Participation), 2009 enhance voter participation.
- Home voting for senior citizens above 85 years and PwDs with 40% disability in 2024 general elections.
- Elimination of duplicate voter IDs (2025) is underway with UIDAI support.
Judicial Pronouncements for Reforming India’s Election Process
- Association for Democratic Reforms (ADR) v. Union of India (2002): Mandated candidates to disclose criminal, financial, and educational details, enhancing voter transparency.
- People’s Union for Civil Liberties (PUCL) v. Union of India (2013): Introduced NOTA option on EVMs; empowering fundamental right of choice (Article 19(1)(a)).
- Lily Thomas v. Union of India (2013): Struck down Section 8(4) of RPA, 1951, ensuring immediate disqualification of convicted legislators.
- ADR v. Union of India (2024): Declared Electoral Bonds Scheme unconstitutional, restoring transparency in political funding.
